Title: Cellular Tracking and Gene Profiling of Fusarium graminearum during Maize Stalk Rot Disease Development Elucidates Its Strategies in Confronting Phosphorus Limitation in the Host Apoplast

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Maize stalk apoplast / fungal accessible extracellular phosphorus pool
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential nutrient for membrane biosynthesis and growth
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to virulence, likely by scavenging phosphate from extracellular organic compounds under phosphate limitation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Expression profiling; targeted gene deletion; virulence assay
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FGSG_06610 is preferentially expressed during maize stalk infection, especially early phosphate-limitation stages. 2. It encodes a putative PhoD-like phosphatase predicted to release free phosphate from organic compounds. 3. Deletion reduced lesion size to about 50% of wild type.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Preferentially expressed during hyphal growth in maize stalk
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Early maize stalk infection; host apoplastic phosphate limitation
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential pathogen target to disrupt phosphate scavenging during maize colonization
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Similarity to cyanobacterial PhoD was noted; catalytic activity inferred, not directly assayed

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Maize stalk apoplast / fungal accessible extracellular phosphorus pool
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential nutrient for fungal growth under phosphate limitation
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to virulence, likely through extracellular phosphate release from organic phosphorus compounds
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Expression profiling; targeted gene deletion; virulence assay
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FGSG_03366 is induced during maize stalk infection. 2. It encodes a putative secreted histidine acid phosphatase/phytase-like protein. 3. Deletion reduced lesion size to about half of wild type.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Early maize stalk infection; phosphate limitation
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential antifungal target impairing phosphate scavenging in host apoplast
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Compared to yeast Pho12-like phosphatase; exact substrate spectrum not tested

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Secretory acid phosphatase; aspartate-based protein phosphatase family
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Maize stalk apoplast / fungal accessible extracellular phosphorus pool
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supplies phosphate for growth and virulence under phosphorus shortage
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Deletion reduced maize stalk lesion size to about 50% of wild type, supporting a role in phosphate scavenging during infection
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Expression profiling; targeted gene deletion; virulence assay
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FGSG_07678 is induced during maize stalk infection. 2. It encodes a putative secretory acid phosphatase. 3. Knockout significantly reduced virulence in maize stalk.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Early maize stalk infection; phosphate limitation
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential pathogen target for limiting extracellular phosphate acquisition
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Catalytic role inferred from conserved domain; direct enzyme assay not reported

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Maize stalk apoplast to fungal cell uptake
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Phosphate acquisition under apoplastic phosphorus limitation
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Strong early induction indicates contribution to phosphate starvation response during intercellular growth in maize stalk
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Expression profiling; RT-PCR under phosphate-depleted medium
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FGSG_03172 is highly induced by 12 hai during maize infection. 2. It is homologous to the high-affinity phosphate importer PHO89. 3. Expression is also induced in phosphate-depleted medium, consistent with phosphate starvation adaptation.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly induced at 12 hai; induced by phosphate deprivation in vitro
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Early maize stalk infection; phosphate-depleted medium
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to disrupt fungal phosphate uptake during early infection
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No mutant phenotyping reported in this paper

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Maize stalk apoplast to fungal cell uptake
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Phosphate acquisition under apoplastic phosphorus limitation
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Strong early induction indicates contribution to phosphate starvation response during maize stalk colonization
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Expression profiling; RT-PCR under phosphate-depleted medium
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FGSG_02426 is induced early during maize infection. 2. It is a PHO89 homolog and likely functions in high-affinity phosphate uptake. 3. Expression responds to phosphate depletion in vitro.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly induced at early infection and by phosphate deprivation
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Early maize stalk infection; phosphate-depleted medium
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to impair fungal adaptation to low-phosphate apoplast

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Maize stalk apoplast to fungal cell uptake
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Phosphate uptake and possibly phosphate sensing under phosphate starvation
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Induced during early infection and phosphate depletion, supporting a role in fungal phosphate starvation response
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Expression profiling; RT-PCR under phosphate-depleted medium
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FGSG_01295 expression increases by 12 hai and peaks at 18 hai. 2. It is homologous to yeast PHO84, implicated in high-affinity phosphate uptake and sensing. 3. Its induction supports the conclusion that early maize apoplast is phosphate limited for the pathogen.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Induced at 12 hai and peaks at 18 hai; induced by phosphate deprivation in vitro
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Early maize stalk infection; phosphate-depleted medium
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to block fungal phosphate sensing/acquisition

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Phosphorus-free membrane lipid biosynthesis; glycerolipid remodeling under phosphate starvation
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: DGTS biosynthesis branch replacing phosphatidylcholine usage under phosphate limitation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts diacylglycerol (DAG) together with S-adenosylmethionine-derived methylation chemistry into the phosphorus-free betaine lipid diacylglyceryl-N,N,N-trimethylhomoserine (DGTS)
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Fungal membrane lipids during early maize stalk infection and phosphate starvation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports membrane biogenesis under phosphate limitation and promotes intercellular invasion/virulence
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes DGTS accumulation and virulence in maize stalk; required for efficient intercellular growth under phosphate-limited apoplast
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene deletion; complementation; constitutive overexpression; heterologous expression in E. coli; TLC and Q-TOF LC-MS lipid analysis; infection assay; phosphate rescue assay
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BTA1 expression is strongly induced at early infection, peaking around 18 hai, and by phosphate starvation in vitro. 2. BTA1 is necessary and sufficient for DGTS production, shown by knockout, overexpression, and heterologous expression. 3. bta1 mutants show strongly reduced maize stalk virulence and defective intercellular advance, while phosphate supplementation restores virulence.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Barely expressed during in vitro germination/hyphal growth; strongly induced during early maize infection and under phosphate deprivation, peaking at 18 hai
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Early maize stalk infection; phosphate-limited maize apoplast; phosphate-depleted medium; exogenous KH2PO4 rescue
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: EF1-A constitutive promoter for overexpression/localization constructs
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for phosphorus-free membrane lipid production under phosphate limitation
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: bta1 mutants impaired under extreme phosphate starvation; earlier constitutive expression increased virulence, possible membrane remodeling effects otherwise not reported
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential antifungal target for designing maize resistance by disrupting pathogen DGTS biosynthesis or phosphate-starvation adaptation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Deletion shifts infection toward intracellular rather than intercellular growth; virulence defect rescued by local phosphate addition

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Membrane lipid remodeling associated with phosphate starvation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes phosphatidylcholine to generate diacylglycerol (DAG) and release phosphate-containing headgroup products
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Fungal membrane lipids during early maize stalk infection
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contributes precursor supply for phosphorus-free membrane lipid remodeling under phosphate limitation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Strongly induced during early maize infection and phosphate starvation, suggesting contribution to DGTS-oriented membrane remodeling, but deletion did not reduce virulence in maize stalk
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Expression profiling; RT-PCR in planta and phosphate-depleted medium; gene deletion virulence assay
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PLC expression rises sharply at 12–18 hai, exceeding 80-fold at 18 hai versus in vitro hyphae. 2. PLC is co-induced with BTA1 and phosphate transporters under phosphate starvation conditions. 3. plc deletion did not significantly affect lesion size, indicating nonessential or redundant contribution to virulence.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Strongly induced at 12–18 hai in maize and by phosphate deprivation in vitro
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Early maize stalk infection; phosphate-depleted medium
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Deletion showed no lesion-size effect, implying redundancy or compensation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Lower-priority target than BTA1 because loss did not reduce virulence in this assay
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Proposed to increase DAG production while PC/PE synthesis genes are reduced at early infection

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Storage lipid mobilization linked to membrane lipid remodeling
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Mobilizes storage triacylglycerol (TAG) to generate diacylglycerol (DAG) for downstream membrane lipid synthesis/remodeling
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports precursor supply for membrane remodeling and virulence under host phosphate limitation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Deletion reduced maize stalk lesion size by about 40%, supporting a role in virulence-associated lipid mobilization
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Expression profiling; targeted gene deletion; virulence assay in maize stalk
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FGSG_03846 is one of two TAG lipase genes induced during maize infection. 2. The gene is proposed to mobilize stored TAG to provide DAG during early infection. 3. Knockout reduces virulence by about 40% in maize stalk.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Significantly up-regulated during maize infection compared with in vitro growth
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate antifungal target affecting early lipid mobilization and virulence
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Assigned as putative TAG lipase; exact biochemical specificity not directly assayed

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Storage lipid mobilization linked to membrane lipid remodeling
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Mobilizes storage triacylglycerol (TAG) to generate diacylglycerol (DAG) for downstream membrane lipid synthesis/remodeling
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports precursor supply for membrane remodeling and virulence under host phosphate limitation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Deletion reduced maize stalk lesion size by about 40%, supporting a role in virulence-associated lipid mobilization
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Expression profiling; targeted gene deletion; virulence assay in maize stalk
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FGSG_03243 is induced during maize infection. 2. It is proposed to mobilize fungal TAG reserves to supply DAG for membrane lipid remodeling. 3. Knockout reduces virulence in maize stalk by about 40%.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Significantly up-regulated during maize infection compared with in vitro growth
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate antifungal target affecting early lipid mobilization and virulence
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Assigned as putative TAG lipase; exact biochemical specificity not directly assayed
